@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/sensory processing / occupational therapy / daily living skills / self-care/sensory sensitivities / product tolerance / tactile defensiveness / sensory accommodations/9-materials-that-help-with-sensory-friendly-products#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does every day feels like navigating a minefield of sensations my child can't tolerate. clothing is the biggest battle. tags have to be cut out of everything — even then, the stitching where the tag was can be unbearable. seams along socks drive them to tears. certain fabrics — anything synthetic, anything stiff, anything with texture — are immediately rejected. waistbands that are too tight, or too loose. sleeves that hit at the wrong spot. collars that touch their neck. we've gone through dozens of clothing items trying to find the few things that work. some mornings we're late because nothing 'feels right' and my child is in genuine distress, not just being difficult. hygiene products are another challenge. regular toothpaste is 'too spicy' or 'too foamy.' shampoo 'burns' — even the gentle baby formulas. sunscreen is 'sticky and gross' and leads to meltdowns every summer. lotion feels 'slimy.' new soaps are met with immediate rejection. i've learned to never buy anything new without being prepared for it to fail. then there's the invisible sensory challenges. the hum of fluorescent lights. the texture of certain foods. the feeling of grass on bare feet. the way certain sounds seem to physically hurt them. other people don't see it. they see a child having a tantrum over 'nothing' — over a sock, over toothpaste, over a shirt. they think i'm indulgent, that i should just make my child wear the clothes, use the products, deal with it. but these aren't preferences. i've watched my child try to tolerate things — really try — and seen their nervous system completely overwhelm them. the distress is real. the sensory experience is genuinely different for them. i've become an expert at finding workarounds, but i'm exhausted from the constant search. there must be products specifically designed for sensory-sensitive kids — clothes without the triggers, hygiene products formulated for sensitive systems, solutions that work with my child's neurology instead of against it?

acceptedAnswer.text: SENSORY OVER-RESPONSIVITY / TACTILE DEFENSIVENESS / SENSORY PROCESSING DIFFERENCES / SENSORY MODULATION DISORDER! Sensory processing differences affect how the nervous system receives, interprets, and responds to sensory input. Children with sensory over-responsivity experience everyday sensations — textures, temperatures, pressures, tastes, smells — as significantly more intense than neurotypical peers. What feels like a minor irritation to most people can feel genuinely painful or unbearable to a sensory-sensitive child. This is neurological, not behavioral. The child isn't being dramatic or difficult — their nervous system is processing sensory information differently. Tactile defensiveness specifically refers to over-responsivity to touch sensations, making clothing textures, tags, seams, and product textures particularly challenging. Sensory-friendly products are designed with these neurological differences in mind — eliminating common triggers (tags, rough seams, irritating ingredients) and prioritizing textures and formulations that sensory-sensitive systems can tolerate. These products are accommodations, not indulgences — they allow the child to function without their nervous system being in constant distress.

mainEntity.name: What is Sensory Over-Responsivity / Tactile Defensiveness / Sensory Processing Differences / Sensory Modulation Disorder?

acceptedAnswer.text: Sensory processing differences affect how the nervous system receives, interprets, and responds to sensory input. Children with sensory over-responsivity experience everyday sensations — textures, temperatures, pressures, tastes, smells — as significantly more intense than neurotypical peers. What feels like a minor irritation to most people can feel genuinely painful or unbearable to a sensory-sensitive child. This is neurological, not behavioral. The child isn't being dramatic or difficult — their nervous system is processing sensory information differently. Tactile defensiveness specifically refers to over-responsivity to touch sensations, making clothing textures, tags, seams, and product textures particularly challenging. Sensory-friendly products are designed with these neurological differences in mind — eliminating common triggers (tags, rough seams, irritating ingredients) and prioritizing textures and formulations that sensory-sensitive systems can tolerate. These products are accommodations, not indulgences — they allow the child to function without their nervous system being in constant distress. Common signs include: Extreme reactions to clothing tags, seams, or specific fabrics, Very limited clothing tolerance — only certain items are acceptable, Distress over 'minor' sensations others wouldn't notice, Strong negative reactions to hygiene products (toothpaste, shampoo, lotions), Difficulty tolerating sunscreen, bug spray, or other topical products.

mainEntity.name: How does a Seamless, Tagless Sensory Clothing help with sensory over-responsivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard clothing is designed without consideration for sensory-sensitive nervous systems. Tags create constant irritation at the back of the neck. Seams — especially on socks and underwear — create pressure points and texture variations the brain can't filter out. Sensory-friendly clothing is specifically designed to eliminate these triggers: truly tagless (no tag residue, printed labels instead), flat or minimal seams, soft fabrics that don't irritate. For tactile-defensive children, putting on sensory-friendly clothing versus regular clothing is the difference between constant low-grade distress and being able to focus on their day. Major brands now offer sensory lines, and specialty companies focus exclusively on sensory-friendly design. While more expensive than standard clothing, the reduction in daily battles and the increase in child comfort often makes them essential rather than optional. Price: ₹800–3,000 per item. DIY option: While purpose-made sensory clothing is ideal, some modifications can help with existing clothing: Cut all tags out completely, then cover the remaining stitching with soft fabric tape or iron-on patches. Turn socks inside out so seams face outward. Choose loose-fitting clothing to reduce pressure points. Stick to fabrics the child tolerates (often 100% cotton, bamboo, or specific blends). Wash new clothes multiple times before wearing to soften fabric. Remove any decorative elements that create texture variation. For underwear, choose seamless styles or boxer briefs with flat waistbands. These modifications help but may not fully replicate purpose-designed sensory clothing. Safety: Ensure clothing fits appropriately — too tight increases sensory distress. Wash before wearing to remove manufacturing residues.

mainEntity.name: How does a Seamless Sensory Socks help with sensory over-responsivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Socks are often the single most challenging clothing item for sensory-sensitive children. The seam across the toe creates a pressure ridge the brain can't ignore. The elastic around the ankle can feel too tight. The texture of the fabric may be intolerable. Many sensory-sensitive children will melt down over socks when they can manage other clothing. Seamless socks are designed specifically to address this — true seamless construction with no toe seam, gentle non-binding tops, consistent soft texture throughout. SmartKnitKIDS and similar brands create socks that many previously 'sock-refusing' children can tolerate. Some children prefer compression socks for the consistent deep pressure, while others need the loosest possible fit. Finding the right sock style can transform mornings. Price: ₹400–1,200 per pair. DIY option: For immediate relief, try turning regular socks inside out so seams face away from skin. Look for socks marketed as 'seamless toe' though these vary in quality. Some children do better with very thin socks (less texture), others with thick cushioned socks (more consistent input). Ankle socks may be easier than crew socks due to less fabric. Some children prefer no socks at all with appropriate footwear — this is a valid accommodation. If child tolerates tights or leggings, these may work as sock alternatives. Compression socks with seamless toes work well for children who seek pressure input. Safety: Proper fit matters — too tight increases distress. Have multiple pairs for frequent washing. Replace when elastic becomes inconsistent.

mainEntity.name: How does a Sensory-Friendly Toothpaste help with sensory over-responsivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard toothpaste contains multiple sensory triggers: strong mint flavors that can feel 'burning' to sensitive mouths, foaming agents that create overwhelming texture, and intense flavors that linger. For oral-sensitive children, brushing teeth with regular toothpaste can be genuinely distressing. Sensory-friendly toothpastes are formulated without the common triggers: mild or unflavored options, low or no foam formulas, gentle textures. Many children who refuse standard toothpaste can tolerate these alternatives, making dental hygiene possible without daily battles. Brands like Orajel Training Toothpaste, Tom's of Maine Children's, and specialty unflavored options provide alternatives. Finding a tolerable toothpaste can transform the dental hygiene routine. Price: ₹200–600. DIY option: If commercial sensory-friendly toothpaste isn't available, try training toothpastes designed for infants (typically very mild and low-foam). Some children tolerate strawberry or bubblegum flavors better than mint. Unflavored options exist but may be harder to find. Reduce the amount of toothpaste used — a small smear rather than a full pea-size can reduce sensory overwhelm while still cleaning effectively. Some children do better with tooth powder or tooth gel instead of paste. Consult dentist about fluoride needs — some sensory-friendly options are fluoride-free, which may require fluoride supplementation through other means. Safety: Ensure adequate fluoride for dental health — consult dentist if using fluoride-free toothpaste. Supervise brushing with younger children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Fragrance-Free, Gentle Shampoo and Body Wash help with sensory over-responsivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard shampoos and body washes contain fragrances, sulfates, and other ingredients that can irritate sensory-sensitive skin and overwhelm the olfactory system. The 'burning' sensation some children report from shampoo may be real — their skin and nervous system is more reactive to common ingredients. Fragrance-free, gentle formulations designed for sensitive skin reduce chemical irritation. Sulfate-free options create less lather (which some sensory children find overwhelming) while still cleaning effectively. Hypoallergenic formulas minimize the ingredients that cause reactions. For children who report that shampoo 'hurts,' switching to truly gentle products can make bath time manageable. Price: ₹300–800. DIY option: Look for products labeled: fragrance-free (not just 'unscented' which may have masking fragrances), sulfate-free, hypoallergenic, 'for sensitive skin,' or 'dermatologist tested.' Baby products are often gentler than kids' products. Castile soap diluted with water works for many sensitive children. Some children do better with solid bar soap than liquid — or vice versa. Reduce the amount of product used. Ensure thorough rinsing as residue can continue irritating. Consider water temperature — some children are more temperature-sensitive and need lukewarm rather than warm water. Bath crayons, bath toys, and other additions may introduce sensory triggers — keep bath simple if child is struggling. Safety: Patch test new products before full use. Ensure complete rinsing. Watch for signs of allergic reaction.

mainEntity.name: How does a Sensory-Friendly Sunscreen help with sensory over-responsivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Sunscreen is essential for skin protection but often intolerable for sensory-sensitive children. The greasy, sticky texture is overwhelming for tactile-defensive kids. Strong fragrances add olfactory overload. The application process — rubbing product all over the body — can be sensory nightmare. Spray sunscreens may help with application but often contain more irritating ingredients. Sensory-friendly sunscreens are designed to address these challenges: mineral-based formulas that dry matte rather than staying greasy, fragrance-free options, quick-absorbing textures. Stick sunscreens provide an alternative application method that some children tolerate better than lotion rubbing. Finding a tolerable sunscreen allows outdoor play without summer meltdowns. Price: ₹500–1,500. DIY option: Look for mineral (zinc oxide or titanium dioxide) sunscreens which often have less irritating ingredients than chemical sunscreens. Choose formulas labeled 'dry touch,' 'matte finish,' or 'non-greasy.' Stick sunscreens eliminate the rubbing application — child may be able to self-apply. Fragrance-free is essential for scent-sensitive children. Let sunscreen dry completely before child puts on clothing. Apply in a quiet, calm environment rather than rushed before outdoor play. Some children tolerate sunscreen better if they have control — let them apply it themselves with guidance. Sun-protective clothing (UPF rated) can reduce the amount of sunscreen needed, making the process more manageable. Safety: Ensure adequate SPF protection (30+). Reapply as directed. Combine with sun-protective clothing and shade for best protection.

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted or Compression Clothing help with sensory over-responsivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: While many sensory-sensitive children are over-responsive to light touch, textures, and tags, they may simultaneously seek or benefit from deep pressure input. Weighted and compression clothing provides consistent, even pressure that many sensory systems find calming and organizing. Compression shirts, leggings, or shorts provide constant gentle pressure without specific pressure points. Weighted vests add calming proprioceptive input. This deep pressure can help modulate the sensory system, potentially making other sensory input more tolerable. For children who are calmed by tight hugs, heavy blankets, or being squished, wearable pressure can provide this input throughout the day. These are therapeutic garments, often recommended by occupational therapists as part of a sensory diet. Price: ₹1,500–5,000. DIY option: For compression effect, tight-fitting athletic wear or dance leotards can work if the child tolerates the fabric texture. Lycra-blend clothing provides consistent compression. Swimwear worn under clothing provides some compression. For weighted input, weighted lap pads or shoulder wraps can be used during seated activities as an alternative to weighted vests. Always consult with an occupational therapist before using weighted clothing — there are guidelines about weight (typically 5-10% of body weight maximum), duration of wear, and appropriate use. Weighted clothing is not appropriate for all children or all situations. Safety: Consult occupational therapist before using weighted items. Follow weight guidelines (typically 5-10% body weight max). Monitor for signs of discomfort. Not appropriate for use during sleep.

acceptedAnswer.text: REAL WORLD EVIDENCE from Pinnacle Blooms Network: 20M+ Exclusive 1:1 Sessions. 97%+ Measured Improvement. 70+ Centers.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/sensory processing / occupational therapy / daily living skills / self-care/sensory sensitivities / product tolerance / tactile defensiveness / sensory accommodations/9-materials-that-help-with-sensory-friendly-products#howto
@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Sensory Over Responsivity
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Sensory sensitivities are neurological differences in how the brain processes sensory input — not behavioral choices, attention-seeking, or character flaws. Children with sensory over-responsivity experience everyday sensations (clothing textures, product ingredients, temperatures, pressures) as significantly more intense than neurotypical peers. The distress they express is genuine — their nervous system is processing information in a way that creates real discomfort or pain. Accommodation through sensory-friendly products is a legitimate therapeutic approach that removes unnecessary sensory barriers to functioning. These accommodations aren't 'giving in' or 'spoiling' — they're providing the sensory environment the child's nervous system needs to function. Forcing children to tolerate unbearable sensations doesn't build tolerance; it often increases sensitization, creates trauma, and damages trust. Occupational therapy can help develop coping strategies and, in some cases, gradually expand tolerance — but accommodation remains valuable throughout life for many individuals with significant sensory differences.

The child isn't being dramatic or difficult — their nervous system is processing sensory information differently. Sensory-friendly products are designed with these neurological differences in mind — eliminating common triggers (tags, rough seams, irritating ingredients) and prioritizing textures and formulations that sensory-sensitive systems can tolerate. These products are accommodations, not indulgences — they allow the child to function without their nervous system being in constant distress. Some children do better with very thin socks (less texture), others with thick cushioned socks (more consistent input). Ankle socks may be easier than crew socks due to less fabric. Some children prefer no socks at all with appropriate footwear — this is a valid accommodation. If child tolerates tights or leggings, these may work as sock alternatives. Compression socks with seamless toes work well for children who seek pressure input.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Proper fit matters — too tight increases distress. Have multiple pairs for frequent washing. Replace when elastic becomes inconsistent.

item.name: Sensory-Friendly Toothpaste
item.description: Standard toothpaste contains multiple sensory triggers: strong mint flavors that can feel 'burning' to sensitive mouths, foaming agents that create overwhelming texture, and intense flavors that linger. For oral-sensitive children, brushing teeth with regular toothpaste can be genuinely distressing. Sensory-friendly toothpastes are formulated without the common triggers: mild or unflavored options, low or no foam formulas, gentle textures. Many children who refuse standard toothpaste can tolerate these alternatives, making dental hygiene possible without daily battles. Brands like Orajel Training Toothpaste, Tom's of Maine Children's, and specialty unflavored options provide alternatives. Finding a tolerable toothpaste can transform the dental hygiene routine.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: If commercial sensory-friendly toothpaste isn't available, try training toothpastes designed for infants (typically very mild and low-foam). Some children tolerate strawberry or bubblegum flavors better than mint. Unflavored options exist but may be harder to find. Reduce the amount of toothpaste used — a small smear rather than a full pea-size can reduce sensory overwhelm while still cleaning effectively. Some children do better with tooth powder or tooth gel instead of paste. Consult dentist about fluoride needs — some sensory-friendly options are fluoride-free, which may require fluoride supplementation through other means.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure adequate fluoride for dental health — consult dentist if using fluoride-free toothpaste. Supervise brushing with younger children.

item.name: Fragrance-Free, Gentle Shampoo and Body Wash
item.description: Standard shampoos and body washes contain fragrances, sulfates, and other ingredients that can irritate sensory-sensitive skin and overwhelm the olfactory system. The 'burning' sensation some children report from shampoo may be real — their skin and nervous system is more reactive to common ingredients. Fragrance-free, gentle formulations designed for sensitive skin reduce chemical irritation. Sulfate-free options create less lather (which some sensory children find overwhelming) while still cleaning effectively. Hypoallergenic formulas minimize the ingredients that cause reactions. For children who report that shampoo 'hurts,' switching to truly gentle products can make bath time manageable.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Look for products labeled: fragrance-free (not just 'unscented' which may have masking fragrances), sulfate-free, hypoallergenic, 'for sensitive skin,' or 'dermatologist tested.' Baby products are often gentler than kids' products. Castile soap diluted with water works for many sensitive children. Some children do better with solid bar soap than liquid — or vice versa. Reduce the amount of product used. Ensure thorough rinsing as residue can continue irritating. Consider water temperature — some children are more temperature-sensitive and need lukewarm rather than warm water. Bath crayons, bath toys, and other additions may introduce sensory triggers — keep bath simple if child is struggling.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Patch test new products before full use. Ensure complete rinsing. Watch for signs of allergic reaction.

item.name: Sensory-Friendly Sunscreen
item.description: Sunscreen is essential for skin protection but often intolerable for sensory-sensitive children. The greasy, sticky texture is overwhelming for tactile-defensive kids. Strong fragrances add olfactory overload. The application process — rubbing product all over the body — can be sensory nightmare. Spray sunscreens may help with application but often contain more irritating ingredients. Sensory-friendly sunscreens are designed to address these challenges: mineral-based formulas that dry matte rather than staying greasy, fragrance-free options, quick-absorbing textures. Stick sunscreens provide an alternative application method that some children tolerate better than lotion rubbing. Finding a tolerable sunscreen allows outdoor play without summer meltdowns.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Look for mineral (zinc oxide or titanium dioxide) sunscreens which often have less irritating ingredients than chemical sunscreens. Choose formulas labeled 'dry touch,' 'matte finish,' or 'non-greasy.' Stick sunscreens eliminate the rubbing application — child may be able to self-apply. Fragrance-free is essential for scent-sensitive children. Let sunscreen dry completely before child puts on clothing. Apply in a quiet, calm environment rather than rushed before outdoor play. Some children tolerate sunscreen better if they have control — let them apply it themselves with guidance. Sun-protective clothing (UPF rated) can reduce the amount of sunscreen needed, making the process more manageable.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure adequate SPF protection (30+). Reapply as directed. Combine with sun-protective clothing and shade for best protection.

item.name: Weighted or Compression Clothing
item.description: While many sensory-sensitive children are over-responsive to light touch, textures, and tags, they may simultaneously seek or benefit from deep pressure input. Weighted and compression clothing provides consistent, even pressure that many sensory systems find calming and organizing. Compression shirts, leggings, or shorts provide constant gentle pressure without specific pressure points. Weighted vests add calming proprioceptive input. This deep pressure can help modulate the sensory system, potentially making other sensory input more tolerable. For children who are calmed by tight hugs, heavy blankets, or being squished, wearable pressure can provide this input throughout the day. These are therapeutic garments, often recommended by occupational therapists as part of a sensory diet.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: For compression effect, tight-fitting athletic wear or dance leotards can work if the child tolerates the fabric texture. Lycra-blend clothing provides consistent compression. Swimwear worn under clothing provides some compression. For weighted input, weighted lap pads or shoulder wraps can be used during seated activities as an alternative to weighted vests. Always consult with an occupational therapist before using weighted clothing — there are guidelines about weight (typically 5-10% of body weight maximum), duration of wear, and appropriate use. Weighted clothing is not appropriate for all children or all situations.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Consult occupational therapist before using weighted items. Follow weight guidelines (typically 5-10% body weight max). Monitor for signs of discomfort. Not appropriate for use during sleep.

item.name: Soft Sensory Toothbrush
item.description: The toothbrush itself can be as problematic as toothpaste for oral-sensitive children. Standard bristles may feel too rough, the brush head too large, the handle uncomfortable. Sensory-friendly toothbrushes address these issues: extra-soft bristles for sensitive gums and oral tissue, smaller brush heads that feel less invasive, ergonomic handles for children with motor challenges. Electric toothbrushes provide consistent pressure and movement that some sensory seekers prefer, while other sensory-avoidant children find the vibration overwhelming. Three-sided toothbrushes reduce brushing time and manipulation. The right toothbrush matched to the child's sensory profile can make dental hygiene achievable.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Look for toothbrushes labeled 'extra soft' or 'ultra soft' — standard 'soft' may still be too rough. Smaller brush heads (infant or toddler sizes) may work for older children who find regular sizes overwhelming. Let the child choose from acceptable options — some control reduces resistance. Some children prefer finger brushes that feel less invasive. Electric toothbrushes: try before committing — some children love the vibration, others find it unbearable. Three-sided toothbrushes (Collis Curve, etc.) brush all surfaces at once, reducing time in the mouth. Oral motor work with an OT can help desensitize the mouth over time, making brushing easier.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Replace toothbrush regularly (every 3 months or when bristles fray). Supervise brushing with younger children.

item.name: Sensitive-Skin Laundry Detergent
item.description: The sensory experience of clothing doesn't stop at the garment itself — residues from laundry products can create ongoing sensory irritation. Standard detergents leave fragrances and chemicals in fabric that may irritate sensitive skin and overwhelm sensitive olfactory systems. Fabric softeners add additional chemical layers. Sensory-sensitive children may be reacting not just to the clothes but to what's on the clothes. Free-and-clear, fragrance-free, hypoallergenic detergents minimize these residues. Some families find that extra rinse cycles help remove more product. Eliminating fabric softener (or using wool dryer balls instead) removes another irritation source. Clean-rinsed clothing in tolerable fabrics gives the best chance of sensory comfort.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Switch to detergents labeled 'free and clear,' 'fragrance-free,' or 'sensitive skin.' Popular options include brands formulated for babies or for allergies/sensitivities. Eliminate fabric softeners and scented dryer sheets — use wool dryer balls for softening without chemicals. Add an extra rinse cycle to remove more detergent residue. Use the recommended amount of detergent (more is not better and leaves more residue). Wash new clothes before wearing to remove manufacturing chemicals. If child tolerates certain clothing items better, ensure those are washed separately from items treated with different products.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Transition gradually if child is accustomed to scented laundry — sudden changes in smell can also be disorienting.

item.name: Sensory-Friendly Haircare Tools
item.description: Hair brushing, combing, and haircuts can be extremely challenging for children with scalp sensitivity and tactile defensiveness. Standard brushes pull and create sharp sensations. The anticipation of haircare can create anxiety that amplifies the sensory experience. Sensory-friendly haircare tools are designed to minimize discomfort: wet brushes with flexible bristles that detangle without pulling, wide-tooth combs that glide through hair, scalp massagers that provide consistent deep pressure rather than unpredictable light touch. For haircuts, vibrating clippers may be less startling than standard clippers, or scissors may be preferred. Finding tools that minimize the negative sensory input makes haircare routines manageable rather than traumatic.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Wet brushes (like Wetbrush brand) have flexible bristles that bend rather than pull — these are game-changers for many sensory kids. Wide-tooth combs glide through tangles more gently than fine-tooth. Detangling spray or leave-in conditioner reduces friction and pulling. Brush from ends upward in small sections rather than top-down in long strokes. Let child control the brush sometimes — self-brushing may be more tolerable than being brushed by others. For haircuts, find a sensory-friendly salon or home haircutters who understand sensory needs. Some children do better with scissors, others with clippers — and some need significant desensitization work before haircuts are tolerable.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Never force haircare that causes significant distress — work with OT on gradual desensitization if needed.

socialMedia.caption: When everyday products feel unbearable. Sensory sensitivities are neurological, not pickiness. Here are 9 materials that help: seamless tagless clothing, sensory socks, gentle toothpaste, fragrance-free wash, sensory sunscreen, compression clothing, soft toothbrushes, sensitive detergent, and gentle haircare tools. Products designed for how their brain works. 🔖 #SensoryFriendly #SensoryProcessing #PinnacleBlooms
